examples.explanation: Start: arr=[3,1,5,8]. Try i=0: coins=1*3*1=3 (virtual left=1, right=1 since arr[1]=1), new_arr=[1,5,8], recurse. In recursion: arr=[1,5,8]. Try i=0: coins=1*1*5=5, new_arr=[5,8], recurse. arr=[5,8]: Try i=0: coins=1*5*8=40, new_arr=[8], recurse. arr=[8]: Try i=0: coins=1*8*1=8, new_arr=[], base return 0. So for arr=[5,8], i=0 gives 40+0=40. Try i=1: coins=5*8*1=40, new_arr=[5], recurse. arr=[5]: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[], return 0, total 40+5=45. Max for [5,8] is max(40,45)=45. Back to arr=[1,5,8], i=0 total=5+45=50. Try i=1: coins=1*5*8=40, new_arr=[1,8], recurse. arr=[1,8]: Try i=0: coins=1*1*8=8, new_arr=[8], recurse gives 8, total 16. Try i=1: coins=1*8*1=8, new_arr=[1], recurse gives 1, total 9. Max for [1,8]=16. So i=1 total=40+16=56. Try i=2: coins=5*8*1=40, new_arr=[1,5], recurse. arr=[1,5]: Try i=0: coins=1*1*5=5, new_arr=[5], recurse gives 5, total 10. Try i=1: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[1], recurse gives 1, total 6. Max=10. So i=2 total=40+10=50. Max for [1,5,8] is max(50,56,50)=56. Back to original, i=0 total=3+56=59. Continue similarly for other starting i's: i=1 gives coins=3*1*5=15, new_arr=[3,5,8], recurse yields max 152, total 167. i=2 gives coins=1*5*8=40, new_arr=[3,1,8], recurse yields max 56, total 96. i=3 gives coins=8*8*1=64, new_arr=[3,1,5], recurse yields max 35, total 99. Overall max is 167 from i=1.

Start: arr=[1,5]. Try i=0: coins=1*1*5=5 (virtual left=1), new_arr=[5], recurse. arr=[5]: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[], base return 0, total 5+5=10. Try i=1: coins=1*5*1=5 (virtual right=1), new_arr=[1], recurse. arr=[1]: coins=1*1*1=1, total 5+1=6. Max is 10.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(n!)
approaches.summary: Try all possible orders of bursting balloons by recursively choosing each balloon as the next to burst, calculating coins based on neighbors. This explores every permutation but is impractical for large n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Burst Balloons
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all permutations of bursting balloons to maximize coins.
ladders.explanation: The Burst Balloons problem requires finding the maximum coins obtainable by bursting balloons in some order, where bursting balloon i yields nums[i-1]*nums[i]*nums[i+1] coins (with virtual balloons of value 1 at boundaries). The brute force approach tries every possible order by recursively choosing each remaining balloon as the next to burst. Intuition: Since bursting a balloon affects neighbors' products, the order matters—we must explore all n! permutations to guarantee finding the maximum. The algorithm works by maintaining the current list of balloons, recursively bursting each one, calculating coins based on current neighbors, and tracking the maximum across all choices. Step-by-step: 1. Define recursive function solve(arr) that takes current balloon list. 2. Base case: if arr empty, return 0. 3. For each index i in arr, compute coins = arr[i-1]*arr[i]*arr[i+1] (with boundary checks for virtual 1s). 4. Create new list without arr[i] (balloons shift after removal). 5. Recursively compute max coins from remaining: coins + solve(new_arr). 6. Return maximum over all i. Time: O(n!) due to exploring all permutations of n balloons. Space: O(n) for recursion depth and list copies. Connection to L1: This uses the recursive sub-routine as its core engine. Common mistakes: Forgetting virtual balloons at boundaries, incorrect neighbor indexing after removal, not exploring all permutations. Real code pattern: def maxCoins(nums): def solve(arr): if not arr: return 0; max_coins = 0; for i in range(len(arr)): left = arr[i-1] if i>0 else 1; right = arr[i+1] if i<len(arr)-1 else 1; coins = left * arr[i] * right; new_arr = arr[:i] + arr[i+1:]; max_coins = max(max_coins, coins + solve(new_arr)); return max_coins; return solve(nums).

examples.trace: Start: arr=[3,1,5,8]. Try i=0: coins=1*3*1=3 (virtual left=1, right=1 since arr[1]=1), new_arr=[1,5,8], recurse. In recursion: arr=[1,5,8]. Try i=0: coins=1*1*5=5, new_arr=[5,8], recurse. arr=[5,8]: Try i=0: coins=1*5*8=40, new_arr=[8], recurse. arr=[8]: Try i=0: coins=1*8*1=8, new_arr=[], base return 0. So for arr=[5,8], i=0 gives 40+0=40. Try i=1: coins=5*8*1=40, new_arr=[5], recurse. arr=[5]: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[], return 0, total 40+5=45. Max for [5,8] is max(40,45)=45. Back to arr=[1,5,8], i=0 total=5+45=50. Try i=1: coins=1*5*8=40, new_arr=[1,8], recurse. arr=[1,8]: Try i=0: coins=1*1*8=8, new_arr=[8], recurse gives 8, total 16. Try i=1: coins=1*8*1=8, new_arr=[1], recurse gives 1, total 9. Max for [1,8]=16. So i=1 total=40+16=56. Try i=2: coins=5*8*1=40, new_arr=[1,5], recurse. arr=[1,5]: Try i=0: coins=1*1*5=5, new_arr=[5], recurse gives 5, total 10. Try i=1: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[1], recurse gives 1, total 6. Max=10. So i=2 total=40+10=50. Max for [1,5,8] is max(50,56,50)=56. Back to original, i=0 total=3+56=59. Continue similarly for other starting i's: i=1 gives coins=3*1*5=15, new_arr=[3,5,8], recurse yields max 152, total 167. i=2 gives coins=1*5*8=40, new_arr=[3,1,8], recurse yields max 56, total 96. i=3 gives coins=8*8*1=64, new_arr=[3,1,5], recurse yields max 35, total 99. Overall max is 167 from i=1.

Start: arr=[1,5]. Try i=0: coins=1*1*5=5 (virtual left=1), new_arr=[5], recurse. arr=[5]: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[], base return 0, total 5+5=10. Try i=1: coins=1*5*1=5 (virtual right=1), new_arr=[1], recurse. arr=[1]: coins=1*1*1=1, total 5+1=6. Max is 10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function to Compute Max Coins for a Subarray
ladders.desc: Recursive function that takes a list of balloons and returns maximum coins from bursting all.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: a recursive function solve(arr) that computes the maximum coins obtainable from bursting all balloons in arr. Intuition: For any list, the maximum coins is the maximum over choosing each balloon as the first to burst, because after bursting one, the problem reduces to a smaller subproblem on the remaining balloons. The recursion explores this by trying every balloon as the first burst. Step-by-step: 1. Base case: if arr is empty, return 0 (no coins). 2. Initialize max_coins = 0. 3. For each index i from 0 to len(arr)-1: a. Compute coins for bursting arr[i]: left_val = arr[i-1] if i>0 else 1; right_val = arr[i+1] if i<len(arr)-1 else 1; coins = left_val * arr[i] * right_val. b. Create new list new_arr by removing arr[i]: new_arr = arr[:i] + arr[i+1:]. c. Recursively compute max from remaining: remaining_coins = solve(new_arr). d. Update max_coins = max(max_coins, coins + remaining_coins). 4. Return max_coins. Time: O(n!) for n balloons, as it generates all permutations. Space: O(n) for recursion stack and list copies. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of trying each balloon first. Common mistakes: Incorrect handling of boundaries (virtual 1s), not copying lists properly leading to mutation errors. Real code pattern: def solve(arr): if not arr: return 0; max_coins = 0; for i in range(len(arr)): left = arr[i-1] if i>0 else 1; right = arr[i+1] if i<len(arr)-1 else 1; coins = left * arr[i] * right; new_arr = arr[:i] + arr[i+1:]; max_coins = max(max_coins, coins + solve(new_arr)); return max_coins.

examples.trace: Call solve([3,1,5]). i=0: coins=1*3*1=3, new_arr=[1,5], call solve([1,5]). For [1,5]: i=0: coins=1*1*5=5, new_arr=[5], call solve([5]): i=0: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[], base 0, total 5. i=1: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[1], call solve([1]): coins=1*1*1=1, total 5+1=6. Max for [1,5]=max(5,6)=6. So for original i=0: 3+6=9. i=1: coins=3*1*5=15, new_arr=[3,5], call solve([3,5]): i=0: coins=1*3*5=15, new_arr=[5], solve([5])=5, total 20. i=1: coins=3*5*1=15, new_arr=[3], solve([3])=3, total 18. Max for [3,5]=20. So original i=1: 15+20=35. i=2: coins=1*5*1=5, new_arr=[3,1], call solve([3,1]): i=0: coins=1*3*1=3, new_arr=[1], solve([1])=1, total 4. i=1: coins=3*1*1=3, new_arr=[3], solve([3])=3, total 6. Max for [3,1]=6. So original i=2: 5+6=11. Overall max=max(9,35,11)=35.
Call solve([2,4]). i=0: coins=1*2*4=8, new_arr=[4], call solve([4]): coins=1*4*1=4, total 8+4=12. i=1: coins=2*4*1=8, new_arr=[2], solve([2])=2, total 8+2=10. Max is 12.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trying Each Balloon as First Burst in Recursion
ladders.desc: The insight that maximum coins can be found by recursively trying each balloon as the first to burst.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the recursive strategy: to find the maximum coins from a list of balloons, try bursting each balloon first, compute its coins based on current neighbors, then recursively solve the smaller problem on the remaining balloons. Intuition: The problem has optimal substructure—the best way to burst all balloons depends on which balloon is burst first, because that choice affects neighbor products and the remaining sequence. By exploring all possible first bursts, we cover all permutations. The logic is: max_coins(arr) = max over i of (coins(i) + max_coins(arr without i)), where coins(i) = left_neighbor * arr[i] * right_neighbor. This is a brute-force enumeration but forms the basis for more efficient DP. Step-by-step: 1. For a given list, iterate through each balloon. 2. For balloon at index i, compute coins = (value left of i) * arr[i] * (value right of i), using virtual 1s at boundaries. 3. Remove balloon i to get a new list. 4. Recursively find max coins for the new list. 5. Add coins to that recursive result and track maximum. Time: Leads to O(n!) due to branching. Space: O(n) for recursion. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function. Connection to L3: It relies on correctly computing coins for a burst. Common mistakes: Not considering all balloons as first burst, incorrect removal affecting indices. Real code pattern: max_coins = 0; for i in range(len(arr)): coins = compute_coins(arr, i); new_arr = remove_balloon(arr, i); max_coins = max(max_coins, coins + recursive_call(new_arr)).

examples.trace: List [3,1,5]. Try i=0 first: coins=1*3*1=3, remove 3 -> [1,5], recursively max from [1,5] is 6 (as computed earlier), total 9. Try i=1 first: coins=3*1*5=15, remove 1 -> [3,5], recursively max from [3,5] is 20, total 35. Try i=2 first: coins=1*5*1=5, remove 5 -> [3,1], recursively max from [3,1] is 6, total 11. Maximum is 35 from i=1.

List [2,3,4]. Try i=0: coins=1*2*3=6, remove 2 -> [3,4], recursively: for [3,4], try i=0: coins=1*3*4=12, remove 3 -> [4], gives 4, total 16; i=1: coins=3*4*1=12, remove 4 -> [3], gives 3, total 15; max 16. So i=0 total=6+16=22. Try i=1: coins=2*3*4=24, remove 3 -> [2,4], recursively: for [2,4], try i=0: coins=1*2*4=8, remove 2 -> [4], gives 4, total 12; i=1: coins=2*4*1=8, remove 4 -> [2], gives 2, total 10; max 12. So i=1 total=24+12=36. Try i=2: coins=3*4*1=12, remove 4 -> [2,3], recursively: for [2,3], try i=0: coins=1*2*3=6, remove 2 -> [3], gives 3, total 9; i=1: coins=2*3*1=6, remove 3 -> [2], gives 2, total 8; max 9. So i=2 total=12+9=21. Maximum is 36.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Coins for Bursting a Balloon
ladders.desc: Calculate coins earned when bursting a specific balloon given its neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the coins gained from bursting a balloon at index i in a list arr: coins = left_value * arr[i] * right_value, where left_value and right_value are the current neighbors. Intuition: When a balloon is burst, it yields the product of its own value and the values of its immediate left and right neighbors (since those balloons become adjacent afterward). If the balloon is at the boundary, we use a virtual value of 1 for the missing neighbor, as per problem definition. This calculation is crucial because it determines the immediate reward for each burst and influences the optimal order. Step-by-step: 1. Identify left neighbor: if i > 0, left_value = arr[i-1]; else left_value = 1. 2. Identify right neighbor: if i < len(arr)-1, right_value = arr[i+1]; else right_value = 1. 3. Compute coins = left_value * arr[i] * right_value. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This computation is used when trying each balloon as first burst. Connection to L4: It relies on basic array indexing. Common mistakes: Using original indices instead of current list indices, forgetting virtual 1s, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: def burst_coins(arr, i): left = arr[i-1] if i > 0 else 1; right = arr[i+1] if i < len(arr)-1 else 1; return left * arr[i] * right.

examples.trace: For arr=[3,1,5] and i=1: left neighbor is arr[0]=3, right neighbor is arr[2]=5. Compute coins = 3 * 1 * 5 = 15.
For arr=[8] and i=0: left neighbor is virtual 1 (since i=0), right neighbor is virtual 1 (since len=1). Compute coins = 1 * 8 * 1 = 8.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Removing an Element from a List
ladders.desc: Create a new list by removing an element at a given index.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating a new list from an existing list by excluding the element at a specified index i. Intuition: In the recursion, after bursting a balloon, we need to consider the remaining balloons for the next steps. Since lists are mutable and we want to avoid side effects, we create a copy without the burst balloon. This is done by concatenating the sublist before i and the sublist after i. Step-by-step: Given list arr and index i, 1. Take all elements from start to i-1: arr[:i]. 2. Take all elements from i+1 to end: arr[i+1:]. 3. Concatenate them to form new_arr = arr[:i] + arr[i+1:]. Time: O(n) for creating the new list. Space: O(n) for the new list. Connection to L3: This operation is used after computing coins to get the remaining list. Connection to L5: It uses list slicing. Common mistakes: Modifying the original list in-place (which affects other recursive branches), incorrect slicing indices. Real code pattern: def remove_element(arr, i): return arr[:i] + arr[i+1:].

examples.trace: Start with arr=[3,1,5]. For i=1: arr[:1] gives [3], arr[2:] gives [5]. Concatenate: [3] + [5] = [3,5].
Start with arr=[7,8,9,10]. For i=0: arr[:0] gives [], arr[1:] gives [8,9,10]. Concatenate: [] + [8,9,10] = [8,9,10].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — List Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Access elements and create sublists using indices in a list.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves basic list operations: indexing to access an element at a position, and slicing to extract a portion of the list. Intuition: Lists are ordered collections where each element has an index starting from 0. Indexing allows us to retrieve or modify a specific element, while slicing creates a new list from a range of indices. These are essential for manipulating balloon arrays in the problem. Step-by-step: 1. Indexing: arr[i] returns the element at index i (0-based). If i is negative or out of range, it causes an error. 2. Slicing: arr[start:end] returns a new list from index start (inclusive) to end (exclusive). If start is omitted, it defaults to 0; if end is omitted, it defaults to len(arr). Negative indices count from the end. Time: O(1) for indexing, O(k) for slicing where k is the slice length. Space: O(k) for the new slice. Connection to L4: Used in removing elements. Connection to higher levels: Fundamental for array manipulation. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in slicing, confusing inclusive/exclusive bounds. Real code pattern: element = arr[2]; sublist = arr[1:4].

examples.trace: For arr=[10,20,30,40,50]: Indexing arr[2] gives element at position 2: 30. Slicing arr[1:4] gives elements from index 1 to 3 inclusive: indices 1=20, 2=30, 3=40, so slice is [20,30,40].
For arr=[5,6,7]: Indexing arr[0] gives 5. Slicing arr[:2] gives from start to index 1: indices 0=5, 1=6, so slice is [5,6].

approaches.name: Interval DP Bottom-Up O(n³)
approaches.summary: Solve the problem iteratively using a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] represents the maximum coins from bursting balloons in interval (i, j), building from smaller intervals to larger ones by considering each balloon as the last burst.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Burst Balloons with Interval DP Bottom-Up
ladders.desc: Complete solution using 2D DP table to maximize coins from bursting balloons in any order.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the maximum coins obtainable by bursting balloons in any order, where bursting balloon i yields coins[i-1]*coins[i]*coins[i+1] (with virtual balloons of value 1 at boundaries). The key insight is to think backwards: instead of which balloon bursts first, consider which balloon bursts LAST in a subarray. For interval (i,j), if balloon k is the last burst, then all balloons in (i,k-1) and (k+1,j) are already burst, so coins from k = nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1]. We build DP table dp[i][j] = max coins from bursting balloons between i and j (inclusive). We add virtual balloons at index 0 and n+1 with value 1 to handle boundaries. We fill DP for increasing interval lengths: for length from 1 to n, for i from 1 to n-length+1, j=i+length-1, for k from i to j, dp[i][j]=max(dp[i][j], dp[i][k-1] + nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1] + dp[k+1][j]). Base case: dp[i][i] = nums[i-1]*nums[i]*nums[i+1]. Time O(n³) from triple nested loops, space O(n²) for DP table. Common mistakes: forgetting virtual balloons, incorrect base case, wrong loop order (must process smaller intervals first). This approach works because it breaks problem into independent subproblems after choosing last burst balloon.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing DP Value for a Single Interval
ladders.desc: Calculate maximum coins for interval (i,j) by trying each balloon as last burst.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes dp[i][j] for a fixed interval (i,j) given the DP table for smaller intervals. The intuition: to maximize coins from balloons i to j, we choose a balloon k (i≤k≤j) to burst last. When k bursts last, all balloons in (i,k-1) and (k+1,j) are already gone, so adjacent balloons are nums[i-1] and nums[j+1] (with virtual balloons at boundaries). Thus coins from k = nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1]. Total coins = dp[i][k-1] + coins_from_k + dp[k+1][j]. We maximize over all k. Algorithm: initialize max_coins=0; for k from i to j: left = dp[i][k-1] if k>i else 0; right = dp[k+1][j] if k<j else 0; coins = nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1]; max_coins = max(max_coins, left+coins+right); return max_coins. Time O(n) for loop over k, space O(1). This feeds into full problem as the inner loop. Common mistakes: using wrong indices for nums (remember virtual balloons at 0 and n+1), not handling empty left/right intervals correctly. Real pattern: for k in range(i, j+1): left = dp[i][k-1] if k>i else 0; right = dp[k+1][j] if k<j else 0; val = nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1]; dp[i][j] = max(dp[i][j], left+val+right).

examples.trace: Interval (2,2): only k=2. left=dp[2][1]=0, right=dp[3][2]=0, coins=nums[1]*nums[2]*nums[3]=3*1*5=15, total=0+15+0=15.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Last Burst Principle for Interval DP
ladders.desc: Key insight: maximize coins by choosing which balloon bursts last in each subarray.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is to reverse the bursting order: instead of deciding first balloon to burst (which creates dependency between subproblems), decide the LAST balloon to burst. For any interval (i,j), if balloon k is last burst, then all balloons in (i,k-1) and (k+1,j) are already burst independently. The coins from bursting k are nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1] because its neighbors are the balloons just outside the interval (or virtual boundaries). This makes subproblems independent: dp[i][j] = max over k of (dp[i][k-1] + nums[i-1]*nums[k]*nums[j+1] + dp[k+1][j]). This works because bursting k last means left and right subintervals are solved separately without interfering with k's coins. Intuition: by fixing the last burst, we decouple the problem. Step-by-step: 1) Define dp[i][j] as max coins from bursting balloons i to j. 2) Base: dp[i][i] = nums[i-1]*nums[i]*nums[i+1]. 3) Recurrence: for length from 2 to n, for i,j defining interval, compute dp[i][j] using above formula. Time O(n³) from triple loop, space O(n²). Connection: this logic is applied in sub-routine for each interval. Common mistakes: thinking forward order, not adding virtual balloons. Real pattern: think backwards to make subproblems independent.

examples.trace: Options: k=1 last: burst (2,3) first gets dp[2][3]=135, then burst 1 gets 1*3*8=24, total 159. k=2 last: burst 1 first gets 3, burst 3 first gets 40, then burst 2 gets 1*1*8=8, total 51. k=3 last: burst (1,2) first gets 30, then burst 3 gets 1*5*8=40, total 70. Max is k=1 last.
Single balloon: must burst last. Coins = nums[1]*nums[2]*nums[3]=3*1*5=15.

ladders.title: Building Block — Interval DP Table Filling Order
ladders.desc: Fill DP table by increasing interval length to ensure subproblems solved first.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the order of computing dp[i][j] values. Since dp[i][j] depends on dp[i][k-1] and dp[k+1][j] for i≤k≤j, these subintervals must be smaller than (i,j). We ensure this by processing intervals in increasing length. Algorithm: 1) Initialize dp[n+2][n+2] with 0. 2) Add virtual balloons: new_nums[0]=1, new_nums[1..n]=nums[0..n-1], new_nums[n+1]=1. 3) Base case for length 1: for i from 1 to n: dp[i][i] = new_nums[i-1]*new_nums[i]*new_nums[i+1]. 4) For length from 2 to n: for i from 1 to n-length+1: j = i+length-1; compute dp[i][j] using sub-routine. This order guarantees when computing dp[i][j], all dp for smaller intervals (length < current) are already computed. Intuition: DP for intervals builds from single balloons up to full array. Time O(n³) from triple loops, space O(n²). Connection: this order enables core logic to work. Common mistakes: filling in random order, not handling indices correctly. Real pattern: for length in range(1, n+1): for i in range(1, n-length+2): j=i+length-1; compute dp[i][j].

examples.output: DP table filled in order length 1,2,3,4
examples.trace: Step 1: length 1: dp[1][1]=3, dp[2][2]=15, dp[3][3]=40, dp[4][4]=40. Step 2: length 2: compute dp[1][2]=30, dp[2][3]=135, dp[3][4]=48. Step 3: length 3: compute dp[1][3]=159, dp[2][4]=159. Step 4: length 4: compute dp[1][4]=167.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Coins for a Burst
ladders.desc: Calculate coins gained when bursting a specific balloon with given neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the coins obtained when bursting a balloon at index k, given its left neighbor value L and right neighbor value R. The formula is coins = L * nums[k] * R. In the problem context, when bursting balloon k as the last in interval (i,j), L = nums[i-1] and R = nums[j+1] (with virtual balloons at boundaries). Intuition: balloons burst independently, so when k bursts, its immediate neighbors are those adjacent at that moment, which are the balloons just outside the interval because all inside are already burst. Step-by-step: 1) Identify k, L, R. 2) Multiply L * nums[k] * R. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection: this is used in core logic to compute value for last burst. Common mistakes: using wrong L/R, not handling virtual balloons. Real pattern: val = nums[left_index] * nums[k] * nums[right_index].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing with Virtual Boundaries
ladders.desc: Add dummy elements at array ends to simplify boundary conditions.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves modifying the input array by adding virtual balloons with value 1 at the beginning and end. Original array nums of length n becomes new_nums of length n+2, where new_nums[0]=1, new_nums[1..n]=nums[0..n-1], new_nums[n+1]=1. Intuition: when bursting a balloon at the edge of original array, its neighbor outside the array is considered to have value 1 (since it doesn't exist and contributes no extra coins). This simplifies calculations by avoiding special cases for boundaries. Step-by-step: 1) Create new array of size n+2. 2) Set first and last elements to 1. 3) Copy original elements into middle. Now, for any interval (i,j) in 1-indexed for new_nums, left neighbor is new_nums[i-1], right neighbor is new_nums[j+1], always valid. Time O(n), space O(n). Connection: this enables basic operation to work uniformly. Common mistakes: forgetting to add virtual balloons, using 0-indexing inconsistently. Real pattern: new_nums = [1] + nums + [1].

examples.trace: Step 1: create array size 4+2=6. Step 2: set index 0 to 1. Step 3: copy nums: index 1=3, 2=1, 3=5, 4=8. Step 4: set index 5 to 1.
Size 1+2=3. Index 0=1, index 1=5, index 2=1.
